digest.top_line: DeLLphi-304: intracranial CNS-PFS 6.5 vs 4.2mo (HR 0.40), tarlatamab controls SCLC brain mets, but post hoc in a mostly RT-pretreated brain.
digest.tldr: Two RT-adjacent reads. Thoracic led: tarlatamab's intracranial control (HR 0.40) in 2L SCLC brain mets reopens the timing of cranial RT, though >70% were already RT-pretreated and it's post hoc. In lower-GI, ctDNA missed 59% of local regrowths after TNT (sens 41%), so it can't replace endoscopic/MRI surveillance in organ preservation.

sites.intro: Intracranial activity of a systemic BiTE raises whether durable CNS control can defer cranial RT in 2L SCLC, but the signal is post hoc in an already-irradiated brain.

studies.name: DeLLphi-304
studies.tldr: In brain mets, CNS-PFS 6.5 vs 4.2mo HR 0.40 and CNS CR 14.9% vs 5.4% favoring tarlatamab over chemo in 2L SCLC.
details: 🔍 Post hoc intracranial analysis of DeLLphi-304 (phase 3 RCT, tarlatamab vs chemo, 2L SCLC); CNS-PFS ITT n=254 vs 255, brain-mets subgroup n=67 vs 56
🔍 >70% of brain-mets pts had prior CNS-directed therapy (typically cranial RT); intracranial activity is on a pre-treated brain, not RT-naive
📊 Ongoing CNS response at data cutoff: 50.0% tarlatamab vs 0% chemo
📊 CNS tumor shrinkage ≥30%: 56.3% (9/16) tarlatamab vs 38.5% (5/13) chemo
📐 Brain-mets HR from unstratified Cox (small subgroup); ITT HR from a stratified model
⚠️ CNS criteria differ across the two analyses: ITT used RECIST per investigator, brain-mets subgroup used mRANO-BM by BICR
🔗 Short median f/u 11.4 vs 11.5 mo (tarla vs chemo); intracranial durability beyond ~1y unknown

verdict.rationale: Post hoc intracranial subanalysis; CNS-PFS not the primary endpoint, brain-mets HR from a small unstratified subgroup (n=67 vs 56), and mRANO-BM vs RECIST differ across analyses.
verdict.audience: Relapsed SCLC (2L), baseline brain mets, >70% prior CNS-directed therapy

studies.significance: The RT read is intracranial control on a pre-treated brain: >70% of brain-mets pts had prior CNS-directed therapy (typically cranial RT), yet tarlatamab extended CNS-PFS to 6.5 vs 4.2 mo (HR 0.40) with CNS CR 14.9% vs 5.4%. Bears on deferring or spacing salvage WBRT/SRS in 2L SCLC with brain mets, not omitting upfront RT.
open_questions: Can tarlatamab defer or replace salvage cranial RT in SCLC brain mets?
Durability of intracranial control beyond ~1y median follow-up
Activity in CNS-treatment-naive brain mets (most here had prior CNS therapy)

sites.intro: Watch-and-wait after TNT lives or dies on surveillance sensitivity, and this read says ctDNA (sens 41%) isn't sensitive enough to carry it.

studies.name: ctDNA in nonoperative management of stage II-III rectal cancer
studies.tldr: ctDNA sensitivity only 41% for local regrowth (94% specificity) in NOM rectal ca; misses most salvageable regrowth, can't replace endoscopic surveillance.
details: 🔍 INTERCEPT program (MD Anderson), retrospective, n=110 MSS rectal adeno with cCR/nCR after NAT on NOM, 2020-2024
🔍 tumor-informed ctDNA (Signatera Exome); 669 serial draws analyzed per-sample; median f/u 25mo (IQR 18-37)
🔍 NAT was TNT in 104/110 (CRT 5, chemo 1); initial response cCR 69 / nCR 41
📊 events on NOM: local regrowth 23 (21%), distant mets 12 (11%)
📊 ever-positive longitudinal ctDNA → worse 2yr local-regrowth-free and metastasis-free survival (log-rank p=0.0002)
📊 first post-NAT ctDNA positive → lower regrowth-free/mets-free survival, but only n=12 evaluable (log-rank p=0.0006)
⚠️ negative ctDNA misses most local regrowths (sensitivity 41%); the salvageable event, so it can't replace endoscopic/MRI surveillance
⚠️ ctDNA+ at regrowth → more advanced salvage path: ypT3-4 75% (6/8) vs 21% (3/14), p=0.01
⚠️ per-sample (n=669) denominator inflates n vs 110 pts; only 29 windows had regrowth, 12 pts evaluable for first-ctDNA

verdict.rationale: Retrospective single-program cohort, n=110 (23 regrowth events); per-sample diagnostic accuracy, not prospective validation. ctDNA's role in NOM surveillance still investigational.
verdict.audience: MSS stage II-III rectal adeno, cCR/nCR after NAT, on watch-and-wait

studies.significance: The organ-preservation read: ctDNA can't replace endoscopic/MRI surveillance. At 41% sensitivity (12/29) it misses most local regrowths, the salvageable event RT-based watch-and-wait depends on. Its real value is flagging distant failure (74% sensitivity) and risk stratification, so keep imaging-led surveillance during NOM.
open_questions: Prospective validation of ctDNA-guided NOM surveillance
Can ctDNA sensitivity for local regrowth be improved?
Optimal integration of ctDNA with MRI/endoscopic surveillance

related_trials.brief_title: ctDNA-Informed Management of Early-Stage Rectal Cancer
related_trials.brief_summary: This is a phase 2 pragmatic study to examine the utility of ctDNA-informed treatment management for participants with early-stage rectal cancer using the Signatera Genome assay. The primary aims are to 1) assess pathologic complete response (path CR) in the ctDNA informed management arm; 2) assess pathologic complete response (path CR) in the post total neoadjuvant therapy (TNT) standard of care (SOC) surgery arm; and 3) assess disease free survival (DFS) in the ctDNA informed management arm.

interventions.name: Signatera Genome ultra-sensitive ctDNA blood test + total neoadjuvant therapy (TNT)

related_trials.eligibility_brief:
Inclusion Criteria:

* Tumor tissue histologically confirming rectal adenocarcinoma that is available for Natera ctDNA assay.
* Cohort A only: Patients appropriate for receiving TNT including chemotherapy and chemoradiation.
* Cohort B only: TNT must have included at least 4 cycles of CAPEOX or 6…
related_trials.answers_question: Prospective validation of ctDNA-guided NOM surveillance
related_trials.relevance_phrase: phase 2 Signatera-guided rectal mgmt vs SOC

related_trials.brief_title: PRODIGE 90 - (FFCD 2204) Neoadjuvant Dostarlimab with Short Course Radiotherapy in a Watch-and-wait Strategy for Microsatellite Unstable or Mismatch Repair-deficient Locally Advanced Rectal Cancer Patients
related_trials.brief_summary: Total neoadjuvant treatment (TNT) including radiotherapy and induction or consolidation systemic chemotherapy has become the standard treatment for patients with stage II and III rectal adenocarcinoma. Along with the improvement of DFS, this preoperative treatment has paved the way to a paradigm-shifting nonoperative management. Indeed, rectal preservation has become a new goal for patients without detectable residual cancer after TNT with the option to reserve surgery for those with cancer…
conditions: Rectal Adenocarcinoma with Mismatch-repair Deficient (dMMR)/ Microsatellite Instability-high (MSI-H)

interventions.name: Biological study on circulating tumor DNA (optional for the patient)

related_trials.eligibility_brief:
Inclusion Criteria:

* Age ≥18 years
* Histologically proven rectal adenocarcinoma with Mismatch-repair Deficient (dMMR)/ microsatellite instability-high (MSI-H). Tumour status (dMMR/MSI-H) should be determined using both IHC (Immunohistochemistry) and PCR (polymerase chain reaction) or NGS…
related_trials.answers_question: Optimal integration of ctDNA with MRI/endoscopic surveillance

image_ocr_texts:
Association Between Circulating Tumor DNA and Local Tumor Regrowth and Distant Metastasis
During Nonoperative Management in Stage H-III Rectal Cancer
Kentaro Ochiai, Megan E. Delisle, Alisha H Bent, Van K. Morris, Arvind Dasari, Emma B. Holliday, Timothy E Newhook, Kristin Alfaro, Kathryn Aziz, Paula Marincola Smith, Michael G. White, Y. Nancy You,
Brian K. Bednarski, Ramy S. Behman, Jeongyoon Moon, Craig A. Messick, George J. Chang, J. Joshua Smith, Scott Kopetz, Tsuyoshi Konishi
The University of Texas MD Anderson Cancer Center, Houston, TX
The INTERCEPT Program
THE UNIVERSITY OF TEXAS
MD Anderson
Cancer Center
Making Cancer Hatory
Background
• During nonoperative management (NOM) for rectal cancer after a clinical
complete response (cCR) or near-cCR (nCR), 25-30% develop local
regrowth and 5-10% develop distant metastasis, highlighting the needs of
improved risk stratification and surveillance.
• Circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA) has emerged as a prognostic biomarker.
but its utility in NOM decision making remains undefined.
Methods
• In the INTERCEPT program, we studied 110 patients (2020-2024) with;
• Stages I-Ili, microsatellite stable rectal adenocarcinoma
> cCR / nCR after neoadjuvant therapy (NAT) and underwent NOM
• Surveillance with tumor-informed ctDNA testing (Signatera™ Exome)
• Outcomes:
1) Local regrowth and/or distant metastasis by longitudinal cIDNA status
2) Local regrowth and/or distant metastasis by the first post-treatment
CIDNA status (within 180 days post NAT)
3) Per-sample accuracy for local regrowth and/or distant metastasis
occurring within +90 days of each blood draw
4) Pathological characteristic of salvage surgery for local regrowth by
CIDNA status
Results
Table 1. Patient characteristics
Characteristics
Conclusions
• Positive ctDNA predicts local regrowth and distant recurrence after NOM for rectal cancer
• Sensitivity of ctDNA for detecting local regrowth is limited
ctDNA should be integrated as an adjunct tool with imaging and clinical assessments
Results
1) Patients with ever positive longitudinal ctDNA had an associated worse 2-year local regrowth-free and metastasis-free survival than those with persistentiy negative ctDNA (Figure 1)|
2)
Among patients with an evaluable first post-treatment ctDNA (n=12), those with a positive result had an associated lower regrowth-free and metastasis-free survival (Figure 2)
In per-sample analysis (n=669), ctDNA showed low sensitivity and high specificity for local regrowth and higher sensitivity and specificity for distant metastasis (Table 2)
4) Twenty-two of 23 patients with local regrowth underwent salvage surgery; cIDNA positivity at local regrowth was associated with more advanced pathological T stage (Table 3)

bookmarks.text: Dr. @g_mountzios #ASCO26 presents CNS outcomes with 2L tarlatamab in DeLLphi-304. Improved time to CNS progression overall (HR 0.54). In pts with brain nets, tarlatamab vs chemo CNS CR rate 15% vs 5% with DCR 78% vs 71% and time to CBS progression 6.5m vs 4.2m, HR 0.40 pic.twitter.com/5i8jL1zlKW
bookmarks.html:
Dr. @g_mountzios #ASCO26 presents CNS outcomes with 2L tarlatamab in DeLLphi-304. Improved time to CNS progression overall (HR 0.54). In pts with brain nets, tarlatamab vs chemo CNS CR rate 15% vs 5% with DCR 78% vs 71% and time to CBS progression 6.5m vs 4.2m, HR 0.40 pic.twitter.com/5i8jL1zlKW

— Stephen V Liu, MD (@StephenVLiu) June 1, 2026

Tarlatamab was associated with a better intracranial complete response rate, duration of intracranial complete response,
and duration of intracranial disease control compared to chemotherapy.
At the data-cutoff date of January 29, 2025, the median study follow-up was 11.4 months (tarlatarnab) and 11.5 months (chemotherapy). + denotes censored data.
*As most patients (>70%) had received prior treatment for CNS disease, specified outcomes were CR, non-CR/non-PD, and PD.
*Assessed in patients with 2 1 lesion that was 2 10mm in diameter.
Cl, confidence interval; CNS, central nervous system; CR, complete response; N1, number of patients with CNS lesions ≥ 10mm in diameter; NE, not estimable; PD, progressive disease.
